title: Cybersecurity & Privacy Readiness

introduction.subtitle: Cyber and privacy maturity assessment. 7-9 min.
introduction.description:
Cybersecurity & Privacy Readiness helps you learn the vocabulary and typical expectations behind Govern & Identify, Protect, Detect & Respond, Recover & Privacy. Use it as a private diagnostic: answer from observable evidence, not aspiration, and treat the result as a map for the next improvement cycle.

This educational quiz runs entirely in your browser. You receive the baseline result without account creation or email.
introduction.privacyNote: Your answers stay in this browser. The result belongs to you. Sharing is explicit and share-safe.
introduction.disclaimer: Educational diagnostic only. Not legal, tax, financial, investment, medical, employment, regulatory, or psychometric certification advice.

questions.text: How clearly are cybersecurity accountability, policies, and risk appetite defined?

options.text: A. Authority is unclear, improvised, or concentrated in one person.
B. The practice is informal; boundaries, escalation, or records are inconsistent.
C. Owners, decision rules, and review points are documented for important cases.
D. Authority, evidence standards, exception handling, and periodic review are governed.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Govern & Identify: How clearly are cybersecurity accountability, policies, and risk appetite defined
conceptHelp.summary: Govern & Identify means knowing who owns security and privacy risk, what assets and data exist, and which third parties or identities matter most. This question narrows the concept to "How clearly are cybersecurity accountability, policies, and risk appetite defined?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Govern & Identify matters because founders, investors, partners, and teams need to know whether this area can guide decisions under pressure, not just sound good in a plan.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable evidence: decisions, customer conversations, metrics, owners, records, reviews, policies, deal materials, or shipped work. Choose a high answer only when the evidence has changed a real choice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current controls for "How clearly are cybersecurity accountability, policies, and risk appetite defined?", assigned ownership, test or incident evidence, and a clear escalation or review path.

questions.text: How complete are inventories of assets, data, identities, and critical third parties?

options.text: A. The information is fragmented, missing, or too inconsistent to support decisions.
B. Partial tools or reports exist, but definitions and ownership are weak.
C. Definitions, owners, access rules, and regular review are governed.
D. Lineage, quality controls, exception handling, and a feedback loop into decisions exist.
Not sure / not applicable.

questions.text: How strong are identity, access, and vulnerability controls?

options.text: A. The area is mostly unmapped, reactive, or dependent on informal trust.
B. The area is known in broad terms, but controls, owners, or evidence are incomplete.
C. Owners, controls, thresholds, and response or remediation paths are documented.
D. Controls are tested, monitored, reviewed after incidents or changes, and integrated into operating governance.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Protect: How strong are identity, access, and vulnerability controls
conceptHelp.summary: Protect means the controls that reduce preventable harm, including access, vulnerability management, backups, data protection, and staff practices. This question narrows the concept to "How strong are identity, access, and vulnerability controls?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current controls for "How strong are identity, access, and vulnerability controls?", assigned ownership, test or incident evidence, and a clear escalation or review path.

questions.text: How capable is the organisation of detecting abnormal or malicious activity?

options.text: A. This is not explicit yet in Detect & Respond; decisions depend on intuition, habit, or one-off judgement.
B. There is a plausible practice in Detect & Respond, but evidence, ownership, or repeatability is still partial.
C. Detect & Respond has a documented practice with examples, owners, and current evidence used in decisions.
D. Detect & Respond is reviewed, measured over time, and strong enough to guide trade-offs under pressure.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Detect & Respond: How capable is the organisation of detecting abnormal or malicious activity
conceptHelp.summary: Detect & Respond means the ability to notice abnormal activity, escalate it, contain it, and learn from incidents quickly enough. This question narrows the concept to "How capable is the organisation of detecting abnormal or malicious activity?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

conceptHelp.title: Recover & Privacy: How confident are you that critical services and data can be restored
conceptHelp.summary: Recover & Privacy means restoring critical services and data while handling privacy obligations, records, consent, and data-subject expectations responsibly. This question narrows the concept to "How confident are you that critical services and data can be restored?" so you can judge the real practice, not the ambition around it.

questions.text: How are critical third-party risks reviewed before adoption or renewal?

options.text: A. No concrete example shows third-party cyber and privacy risk review; the answer relies on intention, memory, or individual judgement.
B. There are isolated examples of third-party cyber and privacy risk review, but they are inconsistent, hard to verify, or depend on one person.
C. Recent examples show third-party cyber and privacy risk review in practice, with enough detail to judge quality and repeatability.
D. Third-party cyber and privacy risk review is repeatedly demonstrated under pressure and changes decisions, ownership, or trade-offs.
Not sure / not applicable.

conceptHelp.title: Govern & Identify: How are critical third-party risks reviewed before adoption or renewal
conceptHelp.summary: Govern & Identify is tested here through a concrete operating question: "How are critical third-party risks reviewed before adoption or renewal?". Use the answer choices to separate aspiration from current practice.
conceptHelp.whyItMatters: Govern & Identify matters because weak practice in this area can make the whole result look more mature than the operating system really is.
conceptHelp.beforeAnswering: Use recent observable behaviour: artifacts, decisions, customer evidence, owners, reviews, or shipped work. Do not score intention as practice.
conceptHelp.example: A strong answer would show current evidence, a named owner, and one recent decision changed by this practice.

questionStageTitle: Learn the concept, then answer from evidence
questionStageDescription: Use the concept button when a term is unfamiliar. Prefer observable evidence over aspiration or ideal self-description.
